questions.text: I feel a genuine moral obligation to act when I encounter suffering, not just to notice it.
I have been able to maintain focus on a task even when someone nearby was visibly upset.
I believe that the suffering of others places a moral claim on me that I cannot ignore.
I approach emotionally charged situations by first gathering facts before engaging with feelings.
I often lie awake thinking about whether I did enough for someone who was struggling.
I can deliver difficult news to someone without being significantly affected by their reaction.
I feel that my capacity to care about others' suffering is one of the most important parts of who I am.
I think that emotional distance is a skill that more people should develop.
When I see someone in pain, my understanding of their experience comes through feeling, not reasoning.
I have chosen not to engage emotionally with a situation because I knew detachment would serve everyone better.
I believe that the world's problems are, at their root, problems of insufficient caring.
I find it natural to separate my personal feelings from my assessment of what should be done.
I am deeply moved by the sight of someone responding to another's distress.
I have been praised for my ability to stay rational when emotions run high.
I feel that when someone trusts me with their pain, I have an obligation to hold it with care.
I recognize that my tendency to remain unemotional in charged situations sometimes makes others uncomfortable.
I can recall specific moments when someone's suffering changed the way I thought about a decision I was making.
I believe that good judgment requires the ability to think about human problems without being consumed by them.
